1: Now this is what you shall do to them to consecrate them, that they may serve me as priests. Take one bull of the herd and two rams without blemish: The ordination sacrifice ensemble: one bull (par ben-baqar = young bull of the herd) + two rams (shnei eilim) — all without blemish (t'mimim = complete/unblemished). The 'without blemish' standard (tamim from tamam = to be complete) applies to every sacrificial animal in Israel's law; defective animals cannot represent the covenant people before the perfect God. This standard has direct ANE parallels: Mesopotamian temple records specify 'perfect' animals for major offerings; the Hittite ritual texts (Hittite šuppi = pure/ritually clean) also specify unblemished sacrificial animals.
2: And unleavened bread, unleavened cakes mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers smeared with oil. You shall make them of fine wheat flour: Three types of unleavened grain offerings accompany the ordination sacrifices: matzot (plain unleavened loaves), challot matzot (unleavened rounds/rounds mixed with oil), and raqiqei matzot (unleavened wafers spread with oil). The three forms of unleavened bread cover the range of grain-offering presentations in the later Levitical system (Lev 2). The use of fine wheat flour (solet) — the highest quality flour — reflects the ordination's sacred significance. The absence of leaven is consistent with the Passover association: the priestly ordination is performed under the same purity-standard as the founding festival of Israel's covenant life.
3: You shall put them in one basket and bring them in the basket, and bring the bull and the two rams: The basket (sal) containing all three bread types is brought together with the animals — the grain and animal components of the ordination sacrifices arrive as a unified offering. This basket-and-animal presentation will be echoed in the Levitical nazirite release ceremony (Numbers 6:15-17) and in the leper-cleansing ceremony (Leviticus 14:10), both of which require the same combination of grain and animal offerings, suggesting a ritual family that includes the ordination as its founding type.
4: You shall bring Aaron and his sons to the entrance of the tent of meeting and wash them with water: The washing (rachatz = to wash) at the tent entrance is the first act of ordination — ritual purification before investiture with sacred garments. Egyptian priestly installation ceremonies included ritual washing; the Ptolemaic temple texts at Edfu and Dendera describe washing rites for priests entering service. The tent-entrance location (petach ohel mo'ed) is the liminal threshold that recurs for all major priestly acts; it is the boundary between the ordinary world and the sacred space.
5: Then you shall take the garments, and put on Aaron the coat and the robe of the ephod, and the ephod, and the breastpiece, and gird him with the skillfully woven band of the ephod: The vestition (clothing) ceremony follows the washing: the garments are put on in order from undermost (tunic/coat) to outermost (ephod with breastpiece), then belted. The dressing of the priest by another (Moses dresses Aaron) creates a ritualized hierarchy: the mediator installs the mediator. The verb labash (to clothe/dress someone else) is used in coronation contexts in the OT (Psalms 109:29; Job 29:14 — 'I clothed myself with righteousness') and for YHWH clothing creation figures (Genesis 3:21 — 'YHWH made garments for them').
6: And you shall set the turban on his head and put the holy crown (nezer haqodesh) on the turban: The turban with the golden plate (tzitz = the holy crown/nezer) placed on top is the culmination of the vestition. Nezer = crown/dedication (the word for both the priestly diadem and the nazirite's hair-crown of dedication); the priestly nezer is the visible marker of Aaron's separation to YHWH. Egyptian coronation ceremonies involved the placement of the royal crowns on the pharaoh's head as the defining act of enthronement; the Israelite high priest's installation uses the same ceremonial structure but replaces the royal double-crown with the tzitz declaring YHWH's holiness.
7: And you shall take the anointing oil and pour it on his head and anoint him: The anointing of the high priest (mashiach ha-kohen = the anointed priest, Leviticus 4:3) with the shemen mishchah (anointing oil) is the act that constitutes him as mashiach — anointed one. The high priest joins the king as the OT's primary 'anointed' figure; both are mashiach (the later royal Messiah terminology develops from the convergence of priestly and royal anointing). In the ANE, anointing with oil was used for investiture of kings (the Amarna letters describe the oil-anointing of vassal kings by their overlords) and consecration of sacred objects and spaces.
8: Then you shall bring his sons and put coats on them: Aaron's sons receive the same basic vestition (coats/tunics) without the elaborate high-priestly ensemble. The son's installation as priests (kohanim) is accomplished through the same washing-dressing-anointing sequence as Aaron's, at a lower level of elaboration. The hierarchy within the priesthood (Aaron = high priest; sons = regular priests) is structurally embedded in the installation ceremony's differential richness.
9: And you shall gird Aaron and his sons with sashes and bind caps on them. And the priesthood shall be theirs by a statute forever. Thus you shall ordain Aaron and his sons: The caps (migba'ot = dome-shaped hats, distinct from the high priest's turban/mitznefet) complete the regular priests' vestments. 'You shall fill their hands' (maleta et-yadam = you shall ordain them) — the filling-of-the-hand formula. The 'statute forever' (chuqqat olam) makes the Aaronic priesthood a permanent covenant institution; the Zadokite priesthood that inherits this office in the monarchy period traces its legitimacy back to this installation.
10: Then you shall bring the bull before the tent of meeting. Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on the head of the bull: The laying on of hands (s'michah = pressing/leaning) on the sacrificial animal's head is the act of identification-and-transfer: the offerer identifies with the animal and transfers their guilt to it. The ordination sacrifices are not brought by the people on behalf of Aaron and his sons but by Aaron and his sons themselves — the priests who will bear the people's guilt must first bear their own. The sin offering bull is for Aaron and his sons' own atonement.
11: And you shall kill the bull before YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting: The slaughter at the tent-entrance (petach ohel mo'ed) is the liminal location for the most significant ritual acts. The entrance is the threshold where the holy and ordinary worlds intersect — the most powerful acts happen at boundaries. In ANE ritual theory, thresholds were the locus of maximum supernatural intensity; threshold-rites (including sacrifice, washing, and anointing) were performed at doorways and entrances in both Egyptian and Mesopotamian religious contexts.
12: And you shall take part of the blood of the bull and put it on the horns of the altar with your finger, and the rest of the blood you shall pour out at the base of the altar: The blood manipulation at ordination: some blood applied to the altar's horns (the four projections at the corners), the remainder poured at the altar's base. The horns receive the blood directly; the base receives the overflow — the altar is marked top (horns) and bottom (base) with the same blood, consecrating its full extent. The blood at the base is the 'foundation pouring' attested in Mesopotamian and Egyptian temple rituals where libations were poured at the base of altars to feed or honor the deity.
13: And you shall take all the fat that covers the entrails, and the long lobe of the liver, and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them, and burn them on the altar: The fatty portions dedicated to YHWH: the covering fat of the entrails (chelev = the pure hard fat surrounding the organs), the liver lobe (yoteret = the caudate lobe or diaphragmatic muscle over the liver), and the kidneys with their surrounding fat. These are the same portions dedicated to YHWH in every peace offering (Lev 3) and sin offering (Lev 4). The fat belongs to YHWH (Leviticus 3:16 — 'all fat is YHWH's'); the burning of fat produces the most intense aroma of any burnt portion, making it the olfactory climax of the sacrifice.
14: But the flesh of the bull and its skin and its dung you shall burn with fire outside the camp. It is a sin offering: The sin offering's unique disposal rule: the flesh, hide, and contents are burned outside the camp (michutz lamachaneh), not eaten. This is because the sin offering is for Aaron himself — the priest cannot eat a sin offering for his own atonement. The burning outside the camp anticipates the Day of Atonement's sin offering disposal (Leviticus 16:27) and, theologically, Jesus's death 'outside the gate' (Hebrews 13:12-13 explicitly cites this context).
15: Then you shall take one of the rams, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on the head of the ram: The first ram (olah = whole burnt offering) follows the sin offering (chattat). The ordering of the sacrifices — sin offering first, then burnt offering — becomes the standard sequence for the major Levitical ceremonies (Day of Atonement, Lev 16; the ordination of Israel's leadership). Atonement precedes dedication; guilt must be addressed before the total offering of self can be made.
16: And you shall kill the ram and take its blood and throw it against the sides of the altar all around: The blood of the burnt offering ram is thrown (zaraq = to sprinkle/throw) against the altar's sides — not applied with the finger to the horns (as for the sin offering) but thrown in a broad gesture that covers the altar's surface. The different blood-manipulation techniques for different offerings are part of the Levitical taxonomy of sacrifice; each technique encodes a different theological meaning.
17: Then you shall cut the ram into pieces and wash its entrails and its legs and put them with its pieces and its head: The complete butchering and washing of the burnt offering ram before placing it on the altar — every part of the animal is used (unlike the sin offering where the inner organs are burned separately and the rest is burned outside the camp). The washing of the entrails and legs (rakhatz) removes impurities from the internal organs and the feet; the animal is presented to YHWH as clean throughout.
18: And you shall burn the whole ram on the altar. It is a burnt offering to YHWH. It is a pleasing aroma, a food offering to YHWH: The first ram = the olah (whole burnt offering) consumed entirely on the altar — the most complete form of sacrifice, nothing retained for human use. Re'ach nicho'ach (pleasing aroma/soothing scent) — the formula for divine acceptance of sacrifice. The formula appears in the Gilgamesh Epic (when Utnapishtim sacrifices after the flood) and the Atrahasis Epic; in the ANE context, it described divine pleasure in the sacrifice's smell. The OT uses the formula throughout Leviticus and Numbers without the crude anthropomorphism of divine hunger, but retaining the concept of divine pleasure in the worshipper's devotion.
19: You shall take the other ram, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on the head of the ram: The second ram = the ram of ordination (eil hamillui = the ram of filling/ordination). The three sacrifices — bull (sin offering), first ram (burnt offering), second ram (ordination offering) — create a complete ordination sequence: atonement, consecration, installation. The progression from guilt-removal to total consecration to specific installation maps the spiritual logic of all priestly service.
20: Then you shall kill the ram and take some of its blood and put it on the tip of the right ear of Aaron and on the tip of the right ears of his sons, and on the thumbs of their right hands and on the great toes of their right feet, and throw the rest of the blood against the sides of the altar: The whole-body anointing with blood: ear (for hearing divine commands), thumb (for doing the priestly work), big toe (for walking in the sanctuary's paths). The right side's prominence in ANE ritual is consistent — the right hand is the hand of power and blessing throughout the ancient world. The blood-anointing of body extremities is attested in the leper's cleansing ritual (Leviticus 14:14,17,25,28) and suggests a ritual family of 'restoration to covenant standing' that uses the same body-points. The ear+hand+foot triad creates a whole-person consecration: the priest is made holy in the faculties of perception, action, and movement.
21: Then you shall take some of the blood that is on the altar, and some of the anointing oil, and sprinkle it on Aaron and his garments, and on his sons and his sons' garments with him. He and his garments shall be holy, and his sons and his sons' garments with him: The final sprinkling — blood from the altar plus anointing oil sprinkled on Aaron, his sons, and all their garments simultaneously. The mixture of altar-blood and anointing oil consecrates both the persons and their vestments at once. The garments are not separate from the priests in terms of holiness — the garments are as consecrated as the wearers, and the garments consecrate subsequent wearers (Exodus 29:29-30). The holiness is transmitted through the vestment-system as well as through the blood-and-oil anointing.
22: You shall also take the fat from the ram and the fat tail and the fat that covers the entrails, and the long lobe of the liver and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them, and the right thigh, for it is a ram of ordination: The fat portions of the ordination ram (same as the sin offering fat list) plus the right thigh (shoq hay'mim = the right thigh) — the right thigh is the portion that belongs to the priests in the wave-offering system (Exodus 29:27-28). At the ordination, Moses waves these portions before YHWH (Exodus 29:24) and then burns them on the altar — Moses does at the ordination what will be the priests' role for all future wave offerings. The burning of the right thigh at the ordination is a one-time exception; thereafter the right thigh is the priest's perpetual portion.
23: And one loaf of bread and one cake of bread made with oil, and one wafer out of the basket of unleavened bread that is before YHWH: One example of each of the three bread types from the ordination basket is taken for the wave offering. The one-loaf-of-each selection represents the entire basket — the portion stands for the whole, creating a ritual economy of representation that will recur throughout Leviticus (the handful of grain offering representing the whole, Leviticus 2:2; the firstfruits standing for all the harvest).
24: You shall put all these on the palms of Aaron and on the palms of his sons, and wave them for a wave offering before YHWH: The wave offering (t'nufah): the sacrifice portions are placed on the priests' hands (palms up/together) and Moses waves them before YHWH. The 't'nufah' motion — waving/lifting before YHWH — is a cultic gesture of presentation, offering something before the divine presence as acknowledgment of YHWH's ownership. In Egyptian temple ritual, food offerings were 'presented' before the divine statue in a formalized gesture; the Israelite t'nufah is a dynamic version of the static Egyptian presentation.
25: Then you shall take them from their hands and burn them on the altar on top of the burnt offering, as a pleasing aroma before YHWH. It is a food offering to YHWH: The portions waved before YHWH are taken from the priests' hands and burned on the altar — the wave-and-burn sequence at ordination enacts the total dedication that the priests will facilitate for the congregation in all future service. At every subsequent wave offering, the priests will receive their portion; at the founding ordination, the founding portion is burned entirely to YHWH.
26: And you shall take the breast of the ram of Aaron's ordination and wave it for a wave offering before YHWH, and it shall be your portion: Moses receives the breast (chazeh) of the ordination ram as his officiating portion — the only priestly portion Moses ever receives (since he is not of the Aaronic priestly line). The breast is the perpetual wave-offering portion that goes to the priest in all subsequent peace offerings (Leviticus 7:31,34). Moses's receipt of the breast at the ordination establishes the precedent for the priests' breast-portion in all future offerings.
27: And you shall consecrate the breast of the wave offering that is waved and the thigh of the contribution that is lifted from the ram of ordination, from what was Aaron's and from what was his sons': The two priestly portions — breast (chazeh hat'nufah = breast of the wave offering) and thigh (shoq hat'rumah = thigh of the contribution/heave offering) — are formally consecrated as perpetual priestly portions through the ordination ceremony. Their consecration at ordination authorizes their reception by priests for all time.
28: It shall be for Aaron and his sons as a perpetual due from the people of Israel, for it is a contribution. It shall be a contribution from the people of Israel from their peace offerings, their contribution to YHWH: The perpetual ordinance (chuqqat olam) establishing the breast and thigh as the priests' portion of every peace/fellowship offering (z'vach sh'lamim). This is the economic foundation of the Aaronic priesthood's sustainability: the breast and thigh of every peace offering brought to the sanctuary provide the priests' food income. The 'contribution' (t'rumah = lifted-offering) is raised up to YHWH and then received back as the priests' portion — YHWH feeds his priests from the people's offerings.
29: The holy garments of Aaron shall be for his sons after him; they shall be anointed in them and ordained in them: The high-priestly vestments pass from Aaron to his successors — each new high priest is ordained in the same garments. The garments' holiness is cumulative: each successive anointing adds to the garments' consecrated status. When Eleazar is invested with the high-priestly garments before Aaron's death on Mount Hor (Numbers 20:25-28), the same transfer of office through the transfer of garments takes place. The garments are the office; wearing them is being the high priest.
30: The son who succeeds him as priest, who comes into the tent of meeting to minister in the Holy Place, shall wear them seven days: The seven-day period of wearing the inherited garments before taking up full service mirrors the seven-day ordination (Exodus 29:35-37). The new high priest's inaugural week in his predecessor's garments is a form of consecration-by-wearing: the holiness embedded in the garments is transmitted through the seven days of use. The seven-day threshold before entering full service creates a complete cycle of preparation.
31: You shall take the ram of ordination and boil its flesh in a holy place: The ordination ram's flesh is boiled (bishel = to cook/boil) in a holy place (b'maqom qadosh) — the tent entrance or the court. The cooking location's holiness requirement means the ordination meal is consumed within the sacred space, not taken outside. The physical containment of the holy food within the holy space is a form of spatial holiness management that recurs in Leviticus (Leviticus 6:16,26; Exodus 7:6).
32: And Aaron and his sons shall eat the flesh of the ram and the bread that is in the basket in the entrance of the tent of meeting: The ordination meal: Aaron and his sons eat the flesh of the ordination ram and the basket's bread at the tent entrance. The meal within the sacred space seals the ordination covenant — the newly installed priests eat their first meal as priests within YHWH's house. Covenant meals in the ANE sealed agreements between parties; eating in the divine presence (as the elders do in Exodus 24:11) creates an especially binding covenant bond.
33: They shall eat those things with which atonement was made at their ordination and consecration, but an outsider (zar) shall not eat of them, because they are holy: The ordination meal components — the atonement-bread and ordination ram — are restricted to Aaron and his sons (no stranger/outsider, zar). The holiness of the ordination food extends to its consumption; eating the holy food of ordination without ordination status would violate the sacred boundary. The zar (outsider/stranger = one without the priestly status) prohibition creates a dietary marker of priestly identity.
34: And if any of the flesh of the ordination offerings or of the bread remain until the morning, then you shall burn the remainder with fire. It shall not be eaten, because it is holy: The overnight burning rule for ordination leftovers parallels the Passover rule (Exodus 12:10) and the peace-offering rule (Leviticus 7:15): time-limited consumption prevents the holy food from becoming ordinary through prolonged availability. What is not eaten within the designated holy time is burned — it returns to YHWH rather than becoming profaned through delay.
35: Thus you shall do to Aaron and to his sons, according to all that I have commanded you. Through seven days shall you ordain them: The seven-day ordination (shiv'at yamim t'male' yadam = seven days you shall fill their hands) creates a complete ordination week. The seven-day structure mirrors the creation week (seven days of divine work producing the ordered world; seven days of priestly installation producing the ordered worship system) and anticipates the Feast of Unleavened Bread (seven days), the Feast of Booths (seven days), and the mourning period (seven days). All major transitions in Israelite life are structured by seven-day cycles.
36: Every day you shall offer a bull as a sin offering for atonement. Also you shall purify the altar, when you make atonement for it, and shall anoint it to consecrate it: The daily sin offering bull for seven days of ordination (seven bulls total) consecrates the altar as well as the priests. The altar must be consecrated (kipper = to atone/purify, then anoint) before it can receive the people's offerings. The dual consecration (priests + altar) in the seven ordination days creates a consecrated priestly-altar system ready to function as YHWH's interface with his people.
37: Seven days you shall make atonement for the altar and consecrate it, and the altar shall be most holy (qodesh qodashim). Whatever touches the altar shall become holy: Qodesh qodashim = 'holy of holies' applied to the altar — the same phrase used for the most sacred zone of the tabernacle. The altar of burnt offering is 'most holy' in a functional sense: anything that contacts it becomes holy (yiqddash). The altar's holiness is transmitted through contact, making it a sanctity-source that consecrates what it touches. The early rabbinic discussion of how the altar's 'most holy' status interacts with individual offerings (m. Zevachim Exodus 9:1) develops this verse's principle.
38: Now this is what you shall offer on the altar: two lambs a year old day by day regularly (tamid): The tamid (regular/perpetual) offering — two lambs daily, morning and evening, every day without exception — is the continuous sacrifice that defines the rhythm of Israelite temple service. Tamid = perpetual/constant (related to the root for 'always, continually'). The tamid later becomes the defining sacrifice of the Temple period, and its cessation is the signal of the Temple's destruction. Daniel 9:27, Exodus 11:31, and 12:11 use the cessation of the tamid as the eschatological marker of ultimate desolation.
39: One lamb you shall offer in the morning, and the other lamb you shall offer at twilight (bein ha'arbayim): Bein ha'arbayim = 'between the two evenings' — the disputed term for the time of the afternoon sacrifice (also used for the Passover lamb's slaughter in Exodus 12:6). The two-evening interpretation has generated extensive rabbinic debate: does it mean between noon and sunset (the Samaritans and Karaites), or between sunset and dark (the Pharisees)? The Passover lamb and the tamid lamb are slaughtered at the same time, structuring all Israelite sacrificial time around the two poles of morning and evening.
40: And with the first lamb a tenth measure of fine flour mingled with a fourth of a hin of beaten oil, and a fourth of a hin of wine for a drink offering: The grain offering (minchah) and drink offering (nesek) accompanying each tamid lamb: 1/10 ephah of fine flour + 1/4 hin of beaten olive oil + 1/4 hin of wine. The wine poured out at the base of the altar is the drink offering — the libation that accompanies every regular sacrifice. Wine as a libation (liquid offering poured on the ground or altar) was universal in ANE sacrifice — Mesopotamian and Egyptian texts describe wine libations to various deities; the distinctive Israelite form uses wine specifically as the 'poured out' accompaniment to the tamid.
41: The other lamb you shall offer at twilight, and shall offer with it a grain offering and its drink offering, as in the morning, for a pleasing aroma, a food offering to YHWH: The evening tamid mirrors the morning tamid exactly — same animals, same grain offering, same wine offering. The symmetry of morning and evening sacrifice creates a liturgical frame for the entire day: the day begins and ends in the presence of YHWH with a complete offering. The re'ach nicho'ach formula repeated for the evening offering confirms that both sacrifices are equally accepted.
42: It shall be a regular burnt offering throughout your generations at the entrance of the tent of meeting before YHWH, where I will meet with you, to speak to you there: The tent entrance (petach ohel mo'ed) as the meeting place where YHWH speaks to Israel through Moses — and now, through the regular tamid sacrifice. The tamid creates a perpetual condition of openness to divine communication: the daily sacrifice at the tent entrance means Israel is always approaching YHWH, and YHWH is always available at that approach point. The meeting (mo'ed) of the 'tent of meeting' is made regular and perpetual by the tamid's daily repetition.
43: There I will meet with the people of Israel, and it shall be sanctified by my glory: The tent of meeting is sanctified (niqddash) by YHWH's glory (k'vodi = my glory). The tabernacle's holiness comes from within (from YHWH's indwelling glory), not from the quality of its materials or the precision of its construction — those are necessary but not sufficient. The kavod YHWH is the true sanctifying power; the tabernacle becomes holy when YHWH inhabits it with his glory. This verse anticipates Exodus 40:34-35 when the glory fills the completed tabernacle.
44: I will consecrate the tent of meeting and the altar. Aaron also and his sons I will consecrate to serve me as priests: YHWH himself consecrates (qiddashtim = I will make holy) both the structures (tent and altar) and the personnel (Aaron and his sons). Human ordination ceremonies (the seven-day installation of Exodus 29) are the means; divine consecration is the reality. The tabernacle and priesthood are holy not because Israel declares them so but because YHWH declares and enacts their holiness.
45: I will dwell (v'shakhanti) among the people of Israel and will be their God: V'shakhanti = I will tabernacle/dwell (Qal perfect of shakan = to dwell/settle); the covenant-dwelling formula that is the theological goal of the entire Exodus narrative: YHWH will live among his people. This is the fulfillment of the burning bush promise ('I will be with you,' Exodus 3:12) and the stated purpose of the Exodus ('I will be your God and you will be my people,' Exodus 6:7). The word shakan gives its name to the mishkan (tabernacle = dwelling-place) and to the Shekinah (the rabbinic term for the divine presence dwelling with Israel).
46: And they shall know that I am YHWH their God, who brought them out of the land of Egypt that I might dwell (shekoni) among them. I am YHWH their God: The da'at-YHWH formula ('they shall know that I am YHWH their God') that drove the plague narrative now becomes the stated purpose of the tabernacle and the dwelling. Egypt was supposed to know YHWH through judgment; Israel is to know YHWH through his presence dwelling among them. The Exodus from Egypt (hotze'ti otam me'eretz Mitzrayim) and the tabernacle dwelling (shekoni b'tokham) are cause and effect — YHWH brought Israel out of Egypt in order to dwell with them. The liberation was for the sake of the presence.
